coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent probes for the rapid detection of profenofos in farmland system
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Feiyan
Zhao, Shengliang
Lai, Xuandi
Fan, Yongyao
Han, Peigang
Chen, Liqiong
coredata.dc:description: Colorimetric and fluorescent sensors were developed for the detection of profenofos. The colorimetric assay relied on the aggregation of cysteine modified gold nanoparticles (Au-cys) composite caused by the hydrogen bond and Au-S bond between profenofos and Au-cys. The further addition of S, N-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) (fluorescence quantum yield up to 98%) into the Au-cys system depended on the change of fluorescence intensity of Au-cys-CDs owing to the inner filter effect between Au-cys and CDs. Under the optimal conditions, the sensor exhibits good linearity within 0.2–1.2 mg L−1 and 20–320 μg L−1, and limit of detection of 21.7 μg L−1 and 5.5 μg L−1 in colorimetry and fluorescence mode, respectively. The developed sensor did not only possess favorable selectivity and sensitivity, but also feasibility of usage in the actual detection of profenofos in farmland system samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Profenofos
Nano-gold
Carbon dot
Colorimetry
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of profenofos in actual samples
Selectivity of the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra assay
Detection of profenofos in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the Au-cys-P (the concentration of profenofos is 1.2 mg L−1) (A); Zeta potentials of the different samples (B); UV–vis absorption spectra of the Au-cys and CDs and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CDs (C); Fluorescence lifetime of the CDs, Au-cys-CDs and Au-cys-P-CDs (D).

Photographic images (A) and UV–vis absorption spectra (B) of the Au-cys based colorimetric sensor against various concentrations of profenofos; The calibration curve of the UV–vis absorption variations versus the amount of profenofos (C). Inset: UV–vis absorption variations at low concentrations of profenofos.

Fluorescence intensity spectra of the Au-cys-CDs based fluorescence sensor against various concentrations of profenofos (A); The calibration curve of the fluorescent intensity variations versus the amounts of profenofos (B). Inset: fluorescent intensity variations at low concentrations of profenofos.
The selectivity of UV–vis (A) and fluorescence assay (B), in which the concentration of each pesticide was 1.2 mg L−1 and inorganic metal salts were 10 mg L−1.
Schematic diagram of dual-signal optical sensor (A) and magnification (B).
Determination of profenofos in spiked real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The 1 mg L−1 profenofos were detected by diluting ten times in fluorometric method. ND: not detected.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent probes for the rapid detection of profenofos in farmland system

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of New Materials and New Energies, Shenzhen Technology University, Shenzhen 518118, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and New Energies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Colorimetric and fluorescent sensors were developed for the detection of profenofos. The colorimetric assay relied on the aggregation of cysteine modified gold nanoparticles (Au-cys) composite caused by the hydrogen bond and Au-S bond between profenofos and Au-cys. The further addition of S, N-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) (fluorescence quantum yield up to 98%) into the Au-cys system depended on the change of fluorescence intensity of Au-cys-CDs owing to the inner filter effect between Au-cys and CDs. Under the optimal conditions, the sensor exhibits good linearity within 0.2–1.2 mg L−1 and 20–320 μg L−1, and limit of detection of 21.7 μg L−1 and 5.5 μg L−1 in colorimetry and fluorescence mode, respectively. The developed sensor did not only possess favorable selectivity and sensitivity, but also feasibility of usage in the actual detection of profenofos in farmland system samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Profenofos (O-(4-bromo-2- chlorophenyl) O-ethyl S-propyl phosphorothioate), a common organophosphorous insecticide, is widely used for the efficient control of insect pests in agricultural products during production (Bodur, Erarpat, & Bakirdere, 2020). However, profenofos residues can trigger a series of problems, such as the pollution of water resources, agricultural soil, and agricultural products, which pose health risks for humans and aquatic organisms (Maddah, Sabouri, & Hasanzadeh, 2017). The toxicity of profenofos on human and aquatic organisms is attributed to the inhibition of acetylcholine esterase, leading to neuro-toxicity and instability of the erythrocyte membrane, which causes respiratory and neuromuscular transmission impairment (Pamanji et al., 2015). In addition, researchers have also found that profenofos can be extremely hazardous for animals and damage DNA patterns and protein structures (Tiwari, Chakraborty, Singh, & Mishra, 2016). Due to the toxicity of profenofos, the World Health Organization (WHO) has classified it as a moderately hazardous chemical (Toxicity Class II). At present, there are still quite a number of countries that use profenofos in agriculture (Bodur, Erarpat, & Bakirdere, 2020). Since profenofos is commonly used and due to its impact on living organisms and the environment, there is a need to develop a sensitive and selective analytical method for the convenient and quick identification of profenofos in farmland systems.

Many analytical methods have been established for the detection of pesticides, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Mahajan & Chatterjee, 2018), gas/liquid phase chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC/LC-MS) (Liu et al., 2020), electrochemical method (Zhang et al., 2020), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Zhang & Pan, 2010), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Bernat, Samiwala, Albo, Jiang, & Rao, 2019), colorimetry method (Rana, Bhamore, Rohit, Park, & Kailasa, 2018), and fluorescence method (Xiong et al., 2020). Methods such as HPLC and GC/LC-MS require large and expensive instrument, complex and time-consuming sample pretreatment, and professional operators (Liu, et al., 2020; Mahajan & Chatterjee, 2018). Electrochemical methods involve a complicated electrode modification process (Zhang, et al., 2020), while ELISA requires expensive biological reagents (Zhang & Pan, 2010). SERS provides the poor selective separation of homologues or components with similar structural properties and Raman activity (Bernat, Samiwala, Albo, Jiang, & Rao, 2019). Comparatively, colorimetry and fluorescence methods provide obvious advantages owing to their low cost and simple and timesaving operation.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) can be used as an ideal colorimetric probe owing to their high extinction coefficient, convenient surface modification, as well as strong size-dependent optical properties (Zou, Mai, Li, & Lai, 2021). The color of AuNPs is red in the dispersed state, whereas they turn to blue in the aggregation state, while the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak of AuNPs shifts to a longer wavelength (Xie, Huang, Li, & Wu, 2021). Based on these properties of AuNPs, they have been widely applied in pesticides detection. Wang et al. (2016) developed single-stranded DNA (ssDNA)-wrapped gold nanoparticles to detect omethoate with a limit of detection (LOD) 0.1 μmol L−1. The AuNPs-based colorimetric method has the advantages of being fast, cost-effective, and convenient, and is useful for semi-quantitative detection using direct observations with the naked eye and quantitative detection through UV–vis absorption spectrometry for pesticide detection. However, the colorimetric method is susceptible to interference from optical backgrounds and is not suitable for the test of colored and complex samples, thus affecting the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection result (Kailasa et al., 2019).

The fluorescence method can effectively improve the sensitivity of the detection result (Nair, Chandran, Mohamed, & Pillai, 2021). Therefore, a dual-modal detection strategy that combines fluorescence and colorimetry can be used to avoid interference effects from the background in complex systems and make the results more reliable (Yu et al., 2021). The dual-modal optical sensor can improve the sensitivity and practicability of the method when applied to real-time rapid screening and detection (Guan et al., 2021). Carbon quantum dots (CDs), quasi-spherical fluorescence nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm (Liu et al., 2009), have attracted tremendous attention due to their excellent tunable fluorescence, photoluminescence properties, high fluorescence stability, good hydrophilic nature, environmental friendliness, low cost, and easy synthetic routes, which make it an ideal material for the output of fluorescence signals for the detection of pesticide residues (Wu, Liu, & Yuan, 2017). In general, there are two main changes in fluorescence emission signal: “turn on” and “turn off”, which owing to the direct or indirect interaction between CDs and pesticides (Liu et al., 2017). Among that, the detection mechanisms of CDs are usually known as internal filtering effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Liu et al., 2017). Based on the quenching mechanisms of IFE and FRET, the fluorescence sensing platform usually contains a fluorescent probe and a nano-quencher, which together form an acceptor–donor pair, and the CDs can undergo fluorescence quenching once the absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps the excitation or emission spectrum of the CDs (Sajwan, Lakshmi, & Solanki, 2021). The absorption spectrum of AuNPs in visible region can overlap with the emission spectrum of the CDs, and thus AuNPs can also be used as an ideal nano-quenching agent (Chen, Qin, Yuan, Shi, & Wang, 2020; Sajwan, Lakshmi, & Solanki, 2021). Chen et al. (2020) established the dual-modal (colorimetric and fluorometric) detection method based on AuNPs and CDs for carbaryl detection with the LOD of 0.06 μg L−1, and the proposed method could detect carbaryl in tap and lake water samples with high precision and accuracy. Sajwan et al. (2021) established a fluorescence method based on AuNPs and CDs for aldicarb detection with the LOD of 3.02 μg L−1, and it was worth mentioning that the developed method was more sensitive for carbaryl detection in spiked samples (cucumbers and tomatoes) as compared to the standard analytical method, LC-MS. The colorimetric and fluorescence dual-modal optical methods based on AuNPs and CDs indeed possess superior sensitivity, feasible and stability, but the colorimetric signal of AuNPs is usually ignored in these dual-modal optical sensor. Additionally, there are barely any reports on the detection of profenofos using the dual-signal optical sensor.

Therefore, we employed cysteine (cys) to recognize target molecules and designed an AuNPs-based colorimetry and CDs-based fluorescence sensor for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of profenofos pesticides. The sensing principle of this dual-modal optical sensor is illustrated in Scheme 1 . First, the Au-cys was synthesized by the formation of the Au-S bond using the one-pot method. In the absence of profenofos, Au-cys were maintained as a stable red solution in the dispersed state. After the addition of the CDs, the absorption peak of Au-cys overlapped with the emission peak of CDs, which caused the fluorescence of CDs to be quenched. When exposed to profenofos, the exposure was recognized by the hydrogen bond and the Au-S bond, which resulted in the aggregation of Au-cys and a change in the color of the solution (from red to blue). Subsequently, the fluorescence of the CDs recovered due to the reduction in the less overlap between the absorption peak of Au-cys and the emission peak of the CDs after profenofos was added. Then, the optimal detection conditions and the influence of interferents were monitored to complete the creation of a dual-modal optical sensor that could detect profenofos. Finally, the feasibility of the method developed for the detection of profenofos in farmland systems was investigated. To the best of our knowledge, the colorimetric and fluorescence probe based on AuNPs and CDs for the detection of profenofos pesticide has not been previously reported on.

Gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), l-cysteine, citric acid monohydrate, ethanol, quinine sulfate dihydrate, chlorpyrifos, bifenthrin, cyhalothrin, permethrin, deltamethrin, copper chloride (CuCl2), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Profenofos, trichlorfon, dichlorvos, and malathion were purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid, nitric acid, and hydrochloric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was obtained from Millipore Co. Ltd. All chemicals used are of analytical grade unless otherwise specified.

The surface morphology and size of the synthesized Au-cys and CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 spirit) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL-JEM 3200FS) at 300 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Shimadzu IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer under a wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1. It was worth mentioning that the sample was added into KBr powder and then dried in a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 3 h to remove the solvent before sample determination. The surface charge of the samples were analyzed using a dynamic light scatterer (Malvern ZEN3700 DLS). The chemical composition of the CDs were obtained using X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) with Al X-ray source (hν = 1486.8 eV) at 12 mA and 15 kV, while the diameter beam spot was 400 μm. The pH values were adjusted using a pH meter (Sartorius PB-10). UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired at room temperature using a Shimadzu UV-2700 UV–vis spectrophotometer with a variable wavelength between 400 and 800 nm. The fluorescence lifetime measurements of the CDs in the presence and absence of Au-cys and profenofos were conducted using a fluorescence lifetime test system (Picoquant Fluo Time 300). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectro fluorophotometer with a variable emission wavelength between 370 and 670 nm at a scanning speed of 2000 nm/min. The fluorescence measurements were obtained in a 350 μL fluorescence cuvette with excitation and emission slit width of 5 nm and at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of the CDs were calculated using the following Eq. (1) (Zhang, Adsetts, Nie, Sun, & Ding, 2018):
(1)
ϕ =
ϕ R
×
A R
A
×
I
I R
×
η
2
η
R
2
where “I” is the emission intensity of the CDs, “A” is the optical density and “ η ” is the refractive index (1.333 for water at room temperature) of the solvent. The subscript “R” refers to a standard. The relative standard used is quinine sulphate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid (QY: 54.6%).

Profenofos levels were determined by a high-performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS). The HPLC was carried out using a Shimadzu LCMS8050 contained an ACQUITY UPLC BEH C18 (1.7 μm) column. Mobile phase consisted of an aqueous solution of 0.01% acetic acid and 5 mM ammonium acetate (A) and methanol (B). The gradient elution was programmed as follows: initially, 90% A, 1 min, 50% A; 2.8 min, 10% A; 5.5 min, 10% A; 6 min, 90% A; 7 min, 90% A. The flow rate was 0.3 mL min−1. The MS was conducted by a multi-ion reaction monitoring system. The electron spray ionization (ESI) source conditions were: atomizing gas flow: 3 L min−1, heating air flow: 10 L min−1, Dry gas flow: 10 L min−1, interface temperature: 300℃, desolventizing temperature: 526℃, degassing line temperature: 250℃, heating block temperature: 400℃.

The Au-cys composite was prepared using the one-pot method. In brief, fresh l-cysteine (1 mL, 0.4 mM) was first added into HAuCl4 (100 mL, 200 mg L−1) under vigorous stirring at room temperature. Then, fresh NaBH4 (400 μL, 3 mg mL−1) was added into the solution for about 15 min until the solution turned a vivid red color. Finally, the prepared Au-cys composite was stored at 4℃ until further use.

The carbon dots were synthesized using the microwave-assistant method. First, 1.14 g of l-cysteine was dissolved in 40 mL of ultrapure water under constant stirring, and then mixed with 3.0 g citric acid monohydrate. After ultrasonication for 10 min, the resulting solution was allowed to react in a microwave reactor at 700 W for 40 min at 110 °C. When the reaction was complete, the reacted product was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water until it cooled down to room temperature. After ultrasonication for 10 min, the resulting yellow solution was filtered using a 0.45 μm PES membrane. Finally, the solution was dried at 60 °C to obtain the CDs product, and then kept in a refrigerator at a temperature below 4 °C until further use.

A stock standard solution of 1.0 g L−1 profenofos was prepared in ethanol and was diluted with ultrapure water to obtain the working standard solutions at different concentration. Then, 100 μL of Au-cys was mixed directly with 300 μL of the profenofos standard solution, and the mixture was shaken for 30 s and incubated for 7 min. For the colorimetric assay, the absorbance of the solutions mentioned above were recorded at 520 nm and 800 nm, and the values of A520-A800 were used to calculate the concentration of profenofos. In the fluorescence test, 10 μL of 10 mg L−1 CDs solution was added to the above-mentioned mixture and incubated for 5 min, followed by the determination of fluorescence (FL) intensity.

Different interferents were selected to test the applicability of the optical sensor in real samples. First, different types of pesticides (1.2 mg L−1), including trichlorfon, dichlorvos, chlorpyrifos, malathion, cyhalothrin, bifenthrin, permethrin, and deltamethrin, were selected to detect the colorimetric and fluorescence signals using the methods mentioned above. In addition, different inorganic metal salts (10 mg L−1), including CuCl2, CdCl2, MnCl2, MgCl2, NaCl, and Na2SO4, were selected to detect colorimetric and fluorescence signals using the methods mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of profenofos in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Actual samples, such as rice, wheat, celery cabbage, farmland water, and farmland soil were used for the testing. First, profenofos was added into the actual samples and prepared to the standard solutions at different concentration. And then, the samples were treated separately to obtain test solution. Finally, sample solution with profenofos or sample solution without profenofos was detected using the colorimetric and fluorescent signals, as mentioned above. The treatment of the actual samples are as follows: celery cabbage, rice flour and wheat flour were purchased from the local market, and farmland soil and water were obtained from the local farmland. 20 g of rice flour, wheat flour or farmland soil and different content of profenofos were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water to obtain standard solutions at different concentration. 20 g of celery cabbage was ground into a slurry, and then 20 mL of ultrapure water and different content of profenofos were added to obtain standard solutions at different concentration. Among them, the farmland water did not need similar treatment mentioned above. Subsequently, all of the solution were sonicated for 30 min, followed by centrifugation at 13,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant obtained was filtered using a 0.45 μm PES filtering membrane. Finally, the pH of sample was adjusted to 7.
To validate the reliability of the method developed, a conventional and frequently-used method, HPLC-MS/MS, was also used to detect profenofos in the actual farmland samples.

The morphology and size distribution of the Au-cys and CDs were studied using TEM and HRTEM (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S1A and S1B, the shape of Au-cys was spherical and monodispersed. Statistics of the TEM results from about 100 particles indicated a narrow size distribution and the average particle size of 6.4 ± 1.0 nm. The insert HRTEM image of Au-cys showed that the Au-cys was highly crystalline with an interplanar distance of 2.4 Å, which corresponded to the Au (111) plane (Biao et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. S1C and S1D, the CDs possessed a quasi-spherical morphology and homogeneous distribution with an average particle size of 2.0 ± 0.4 nm. Most CDs were amorphous in nature and only a few CDs had a measurable crystallinity with an interplanar distance of 2.1 Å, which corresponded to the (101) plane of graphitic carbon (Sarkar, Bohidar, & Solanki, 2018).

To study details of the binding between Au and cys, a FTIR test was carried out. The FTIR spectra of cys and Au-cys were shown in Fig. S2A and the broad absorption band at 2555 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of SH, as found in pure cys but not in Au-cys, which demonstrated that the cys was successfully modified on the surface of the Au nanoparticle by the SH group (Cappellari et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. S2B, the FTIR spectrum exhibited a strong absorption band at around 3440 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH (Chen, Zhao, Hu, Sun, & Yang, 2017). The wide absorption bands at 2996 cm−1 and 2582 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CH and SH, respectively, while the formation of CS, CN, and CO bonds were evidenced by the absorption peaks around 1224 cm−1 (Zhang, Adsetts, Nie, Sun, & Ding, 2018). The weak peaks located at 1650 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the CC bond, indicating the presence of a graphite structure (Xia, Hai, Chen, & Wang, 2017). The sharp bands at 1718, 1517 and 1404 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrational absorption bands of CO in COOH and OC-NH, respectively (Zhang, Adsetts, Nie, Sun, & Ding, 2018). Based on the FTIR results of the CDs, it was evident that sulfur and nitrogen are not only functional on the surface of the CDs but also in the lattice of the graphite carbon skeleton.

To further analyze the composition of the CDs, the XPS spectra were recorded. The full-scan XPS spectrum of the CDs was shown in Fig. S3. The four predominant peaks at 163.2, 284.7, 400.9 and 531.3 eV corresponded to the binding energies of S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, indicating that the prepared CDs were composed of four elements: C, O, N, and S. As shown in the inset of Fig. S3, the CDs contained 63.8 at% carbon, 9.4 at% oxygen, 23.8 at% nitrogen, and 3.0 at% sulphur. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s was shown in Fig. S4A, and the peak of C 1s could be resolved into three component peaks, which corresponded to CO and CN at 288.1 eV, sp3 C in CO, CN, and CS at 286.1 eV, and sp2 C in CC at 284.7 eV (Li et al., 2015). The XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. S4B) could be divided into two peaks at 531.8 and 530.7 eV, which were identified as CO/NO and CO/NO, respectively (Zhang, Hu, Wang, Song, Liu, Li, et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. S4C, in the XPS spectrum of N 1s, the two peaks corresponded to the binding energies of 401.2 and 399.9 eV, which were attributed to ON-C and N-(C)3/HN-(C)2, respectively (Qu et al., 2013). The XPS spectrum of S 2p, as shown in Fig. S4D, could be resolved into two peaks at 164.1 and 163.0 eV, which were identified as S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 (Han et al., 2015). These results further confirmed that the prepared CDs were modified with many carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine and sulfhydryl functional groups, as shown by the FTIR results.

The functional groups of the CDs conferred different optical characteristics in the prepared CDs. Generally, most microwave-assisted methods use household microwave ovens for CDs synthesis, and the control of synthesis conditions is not precise enough, while the QY of the CDs is relatively low (Liu et al., 2018). Surprisingly, the quantum yield of CDs synthesized by a microwave reactor was about 98% in aqueous solution using quinine sulfate as a reference. The ultrahigh quantum yield was ascribed to doping with a certain amount of N and S heteroatoms (Li, Wang, Fu, & Cui, 2017). The comparison of CDs with different methods was shown in Table S1. Compared with other methods, the microwave-assisted method could significantly shorten the synthesis time and save time and energy. In addition, for the microwave-assisted method, synthesize CDs using a microwave reactor had a significant advantage in fluorescence quantum yield compared with a domestic microwave oven, which was owing to a more precise synthesis condition through a microwave reactor. As shown in the insert of Fig. S5, the CDs had a strong blue emission upon exposure to the UV-lamp. The CDs showed an excitation-independent fluorescence behavior in the excitation range of 300–380 nm (Fig. S5) with an emission maximum of 425 nm. A significant shift was not observed under any excitation wavelength, which might be ascribed to the well-distributed size of the CDs (Xia, Hai, Chen, & Wang, 2017). This FL result was consistent with TEM results.

As presented in scheme 1, profenofos induced a reunion mechanism in Au-cys, which was investigated. Before adding profenofos, the Au-cys exhibited good stability. When profenofos was added to Au-cys, the profenofos molecule was adsorbed onto the surface of Au-cys and induced an Au-cys reunion. This mechanism was verified using TEM. Compared with Fig. S1A, the Au-cys aggregated together when profenofos was added (Fig. 1 A). In addition, the absorption peak of Au-cys-P decreased and showed a red shift, while the color of the Au-cys solution changed from red to blue (Fig. S6). To study the interaction between profenofos and Au-cys, a DLS test was performed and the results are shown in Fig. 1B. The Au-cys had a strong negative charge (−20.3 mV), while the Au-cys-P possessed a weak negative charge (−6.6 mV). Therefore, it might be deduced that the reaction of the negatively charged COOH on Au-cys caused a decrease in the negative charge of Au-cys-P. Therefore, the profenofos may have interacted with COOH in the Au-cys through a hydrogen bond. The hydrogen bond could be proved by FTIR test (Shen et al., 2016). According to Fig. S2A, the broad absorption band of Au-cys at 3442 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of NH and OH. As shown in Fig. S7A, the pure profenofos had no absorption peak in the region from 3000 cm−1 to 3650 cm−1. To prove the existence of hydrogen bond, the high (10 mg L−1) and low (10 μg L−1) content of profenofos were added to Au-cys to obtain two Au-cys-P samples, the hydroxyl stretching vibration absorption band shifted to lower wavenumber as shown in Fig. S7B, thus, it might be confirmed the formation of hydrogen-bonding between Au-cys and profenofos. In addition, profenofos contained an S bond, which made it easy to form an Au-S bond with Au-cys, inducing the agglomeration of gold particles. In conclusion, the color change could be observed visually and could be quantified using colorimetric and UV–vis assays.

The absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the Au-cys and CDs were shown in Fig. 1C. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs located at 350 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO bond, which had a prominent impact on the FL intensity spectra of the CDs (Sri, Kumar, Panda, & Solanki, 2018). Based on the absorption spectra of CDs, the excitation spectrum of the CDs was also 350 nm. In addition, the absorption spectrum of Au-cys had a strong SPR peak at 520 nm, which partially overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs, indicating that conditions were suitable for the construction of the IFE based system. To investigate the IFE quenching mechanism of the CDs, the lifetime analysis of the CDs in the presence of Au-cys and profenofos was tested and the results were shown in Fig. 1D. The fluorescence lifetime of the CDs, Au-cys-CDs, and Au-cys-P-CDs were found to be 9.96 ns, 9.70 ns, and 9.95 ns, respectively. The values of the fluorescence lifetime had barely changed, confirming that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the CDs, which was recognized through the IFE of the Au nanoparticles (Wang et al., 2019). In addition, the zeta potential of CDs was −25.8 mV (Fig. 1B), while Au-cys also had a negatively charged zeta potential of −20.3 mV. Since both substances were negatively charged, they remained far enough apart due to the electrostatic repulsion between them. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity and peak position of CDs and CDs-P did not show any significant changes (Fig. S8), which demonstrated that there were no interactions between the CDs and profenofos. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs was IFE, and not FRET (Chen, Qin, Yuan, Shi, & Wang, 2020), which was consistent with the results of the fluorescence lifetime. Fig. S8 showed that the dispersed Au-cys could effectively quench the fluorescence of the CDs through IFE in the absence of profenofos, due to the favorable overlapping of the absorption spectrum of Au-cys with the emission spectrum of the CDs. However, the fluorescence decrease of the CDs was induced by the aggregation of Au-cys, which was relatively low in the presence of profenofos. Therefore, the change in fluorescent intensity could be detected by performing a fluorescence assay. Based on the detection mechanism, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-modal method were successfully established for the detection of profenofos.

A series of experiments were performed to determine the optimized conditions of the colorimetric assay. Generally, pH played an important role during the detection of profenofos, which affected the aggregation behavior of Au-cys and subsequently affected absorbance. First, the effect of pH on the absorbance of Au-cys was studied. It was found that in the range of pH 3–11 Au-cys was stable (Fig. S9) and the color of solution was red (Fig. S10). Therefore, a pH of (3–11) was selected for further study in the presence of profenofos, as shown in Fig. S9. The changes in the pH produced a distinctly different absorbance after 1.4 mg L−1 profenofos was added. At pH (3–5), the color of the Au-cys-P solution changed from red to blue, indicating that the aggregation of Au-cys occurred as profenofos was added. Along with the increase in the pH from 5 to 9, SPR absorption gradually increased, while the color of the Au-cys-P solution gradually changed from blue to purple and finally to red. The SPR absorption and color of Au-cys-P remained almost unchanged when the pH value was between 9 and 11. Therefore, the optimal pH of Au-cys-P could be set at 3–5 in the following study. Profenofos, as one of organophosphorus pesticides, could be easily degraded under an alkaline environment, whereas it could exist stably under neutral and acidic conditions. Therefore, it was unfavorable for the identification of profenofos by Au-cys under alkaline conditions. The effect of incubation time, another significant factor, was also investigated and was shown in Fig. S11. The interaction between Au-cys and profenofos reached equilibrium in 7 min. Therefore, the optimal incubation time was set at 7 min.

In the fluorescence assay, the influence of the amount of CDs, pH and incubation time of the CDs were examined. Profenofos could induce an obvious fluorescence recovery in the presence of more than 10 mg L−1 CDs (Fig. S12). Previous studies have determined that the optimal pH range for Au-cys to respond to profenofos is 3–5 in colorimetric assay, so a pH range of 3–5 was selected to study the effect of pH on the fluorescence quenching of the CDs. As shown in Fig. S13, the value of fluorescence recovery increased as the pH increased from 3 to 3.5, while a negligible change of fluorescence intensity was exhibited in the range of pH 3.5–5, which may have been caused by the functional groups on the CDs (Sahu, Behera, Maiti, & Mohapatra, 2012). Considering that the initial pH value of Au-cys was 3.5 and that the pH value of Au-cys-P was about 4 after adding profenofos or ultrapure water, the pH of system solution is in the range of 3.5–5, and thus it might not be necessary to adjust the pH in system solution. To study the optimal incubation time to quench the fluorescence of CDs, the fluorescence intensity of Au-cys-CDs was recorded in the presence and absence of profenofos under different incubation times. As shown in Fig. S14, an incubation time of 5 min was found to be sufficient for Au-cys and Au-cys-P to quench the fluorescence of the CDs. In conclusion, the amount of CDs (10 mg L−1), pH (4) and incubation time (5 min) were obtained based on the results of the fluorescence spectrum assay.

The fluorescence intensity of the Au-cys-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of profenofos was studied under optimal experimental conditions. The fluorescence intensity increased along with the increase in profenofos concentration (Fig. 3 A). As presented in Fig. 3B, the relationship between the increase in fluorescence intensity ΔFL (ΔFL = FL-FL0) and the concentration of profenofos was outlined, where FL and FL0 represented the fluorescence intensity of Au-cys-CDs in the presence and absence of profenofos, respectively. The linear regression equation was ΔFL = 0.2471 C profenofos − 0.0799 (R2 = 0.990). The linear range was determined from 20 to 320 μg L−1 with a detection limit of 5.5 μg L−1, which was superior to the UV–vis spectrum assay (21.7 μg L−1). Moreover, previous reports on the different detection methods for profenofos were compared with the newly developed method (Table S2). This fluorescence sensor was comparable or superior to that of other reported detection methods. Additionally, this colorimetric and fluorescence optical assay performed under a wider quantifiable range than that of a single-signal optical assay, which is due to the combination of colorimetric and fluorescent detection abilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra assay
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the UV–vis and fluorescent optical sensor for profenofos was evaluated by adding various pesticides, including trichlorfon, dichlorvos, chlorpyrifos, malathion, cyhalothrin, bifenthrin, permethrin, and deltamethrin, and inorganic metal salts, including CuCl2, CdCl2, MnCl2, MgCl2, NaCl, and Na2SO4, individually or together with profenofos (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4A, the decrease in the absorbance upon the addition of 1.2 mg L−1 profenofos was far lower than the addition of equal amounts of control pesticides or 10 mg L−1 inorganic metal salts. Meanwhile, the target profenofos pesticide could be obviously distinguished from other interferents based on the color of the solution, as presented in Fig. S15. However, upon further addition of 1.2 mg L−1 profenofos to the system, obvious and similar absorbance changes were observed. The color of all interferents turned into purple, similar to that of profenofos (Fig. S16). As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence recovery of the Au-cys-CDs sensor upon the addition of profenofos was far higher than the addition of the other interferents. When profenofos and other individual interferents were simultaneously added to the system, the fluorescence intensity of the Au-cys-CDs could recover and reach levels were similar to the addition of profenofos alone. It was observed that all other pesticides or inorganic metal salts exhibited a slight or negligible interference during the detection of profenofos, indicating the good selectivity of the UV–vis and fluorescent optical sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of profenofos in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practical applicability of the developed optical sensors in a farmland system, profenofos was detected in actual farmland samples of rice, wheat, celery cabbage, farmland soil and farmland water. As shown in Table 1 , profenofos was not detected in those original samples. After adding certain amounts of profenofos standard solution into these real samples, the colorimetric and fluorescent optical sensors performed good recoveries. The recovery of the colorimetric method was in the range of 80.4%–100.4%, and the value of RSD was in the range of 2.0%–5.3%. As for the fluorescent method, the recovery was in the range of 82.3%–103.5%, and the value of RSD was in the range of 2.1%–5.1%. Furthermore, in order to examine the performance of the newly developed method, we used it to compare with the result of conventional and frequently-used approaches (HPLC-MS/MS analyses). The quite low concentration of profenofos was all detected through the newly developed method and HPLC-MS/MS method. It could be demonstrated that the new developed method had good precision and accuracy. Therefore, the developed sensor could be used as an alternative method on actual farmland samples for the detection of profenofos.

In conclusion, Au-cys (favorable specificity to profenofos) and CDs (fluorescence quantum yield up to 98%) were successfully prepared. Au-cys acted as both a colorimetric probe and fluorescent quencher, while CDs served as fluorescent probes. Based on the hydrogen bond and Au-S bond induced aggregation between profenofos and Au-cys and the IFE between Au-cys and CDs, novel and simple colorimetric and fluorescent optical sensors were developed for the rapid and accurate detection of profenofos. The dual-modal optical sensor exhibited good linearity within the ranges of 0.2–1.2 mg L−1 and 20–320 μg L−1, with the limit of detection of 21.7 μg L−1 and 5.5 μg L−1 in colorimetry and fluorescence modes, respectively. The developed sensor did not only possess high selectivity against other control pesticides and inorganic metal salts, but also feasibility of usage in a real farmland system. Therefore, the Au-cys-CDs could be developed as colorimetric and fluorescent probes for the detection of profenofos and demonstrated a high potential for real-world applications in food safety and environmental monitoring. Moreover, the colorimetric and fluorescent optical sensors may offer an alternative approach for the rapid, sensitive and selective detection of other targets of interest in real-world samples.

Feiyan Liu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Shengliang Zhao: Validation, Visualization. Xuandi Lai: Formal analysis, Project administration. Yongyao Fan: Resources, Software. Peigang Han: Conceptualization, Methodology. Liqiong Chen: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Key Field (Rural Revitalization) Project of Colleges and Universities in Guangdong Province, China (2020ZDZX1009), Basic Research Project of Shenzhen Natural Science Foundation (JCYJ20190813103601660), and Shenzhen Technology University Self-Made Experimental Equipment Project (2020XZY009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.133321.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
